Sri Mukhalingam

# Sri Mukhalingam

[](https://substack.com/@sadashree)

![Ratnakar Sadasyula's avatar](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!R73I!,w_36,h_36,c_fill,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2F81a2c542-8b3f-4ade-98d2-a40950cffbe3_2640x1980.jpeg)

[Ratnakar Sadasyula](https://substack.com/@sadashree)

Feb 04, 2026

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

6

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

1

Share

Srimukhalingam isn’t just a temple, it’s a living manifestation of the Eastern Ganga dynasty’s spiritual and political identity. The architectural triad there of the *Madhukeswara*, *Someswara*, and *Bhimeswara* shrines, is like a cosmic axis that held the region’s Shaiva faith together for centuries.

[](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!Mlo5!,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2Fb94098de-50d9-4702-a44b-bba4afb87255_900x506.png)

![Image](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!Mlo5!,w_1456,c_limit,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2Fb94098de-50d9-4702-a44b-bba4afb87255_900x506.png "Image")

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

The presence of Lakulisa is especially profound. His iconograph, usually depicted with a yogic staff and surrounded by four disciple, anchors the temple in the *Pashupata Shaiva* tradition, which emphasized asceticism, yogic power, and inner realization.

And how poetic is it that after Chodaganga Deva moved the capital to Cuttack to construct the great Jagannath temple at Puri, Srimukhalingam’s luminous past lingered in its stone mandapas, not forgotten but folded into sacred memory.

The Pashupata tradition, formalized by Lakulisa and his four disciples, held sway over temple-building communities, especially during the Maitraka and early Kalachuri periods. In Mukhalingam’s context, the Eastern Gangas likely absorbed these currents through cultural exchange from Odisha’s Ratnagiri and Udayagiri, where Shaiva-Tantric matrices were already thriving.

Of the Shaiva temples in Mukhalingam, the 3 most famous ones are Srimukhalingam, Bhimeswara Swamy and Someswara Swamy. Built in typical Kalinga style, the temples are noted for their rich sculpture.

[](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!Q7bz!,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fbucketeer-e05bbc84-baa3-437e-9518-adb32be77984.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2F1e4deb8b-1cf4-4267-ab4f-b669aba9530c_250x312.jpeg)

![](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!Q7bz!,w_1456,c_limit,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fbucketeer-e05bbc84-baa3-437e-9518-adb32be77984.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2F1e4deb8b-1cf4-4267-ab4f-b669aba9530c_250x312.jpeg)

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

The very idea of Mukhalinga where Shiva’s face is not merely invoked but visually manifest, strikes at the heart of sākṣātkāra. It’s no wonder the darshan there is likened to the purifying grace of Kashi, Srimukhalingam becomes a southern counterpoint to the sacred north, stitching together the subcontinent through shared cosmic symbolism.

[](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!xC7U!,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2F8dec303d-2336-424b-a09f-a53d84b1dc52_680x510.png)

![Image](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!xC7U!,w_1456,c_limit,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2F8dec303d-2336-424b-a09f-a53d84b1dc52_680x510.png "Image")

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

The triangular spatial layout of the Madhukeswara, Bhimeswara, and Someswara temples is more than architectural, it mirrors the *trikona*, a potent yantric form in Tantric Shaivism.

Madhukeswara anchoring the east, Bhimeswara to the south and Someswara aligned with lunar tranquility and rejuvenation, representing the triple aspects of Shiva: creator, destroyer, and protector.

The fact that Srimukhalingeswara, the presiding deity and namesake of the village, is housed in the Someswara Temple, rather than the centrally positioned Madhukeswara, suggests a deeper layer of ritual or theological intention. It might hint at a shift in patronage patterns, where the temple originally dedicated to Soma, absorbed the Srimukhalingeswara cult.

Or perhaps an organic evolution of local belief, where the temple housing the Mukhalinga became paramount in sacred geography, regardless of its original dedication.

The temples are veritable open-air epics chiseled in stone. Some standout features include Narrative friezes depicting episodes from the *Shiva Purana*, seamlessly interwoven with local lore. Erotic panels in the *maithuna* motif, reminiscent of Konark and Bhubaneswar, emphasizing regeneration and divine union.Graceful nṛtta karana poses and celestial dancers frozen mid-spi, so dynamic, it feels like they might leap off the wall.

Some unique motifs to look for include,the Lakulisa panel, deeply significant for its role in anchoring the Pashupata Shaiva lineage. Makara toranas, their curling bodies guarding sanctums with mythic grace. Miniature shikhara motifs carved into larger plinths, almost like an architectural fractal,temples within temples.

[](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!_iCd!,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fbucketeer-e05bbc84-baa3-437e-9518-adb32be77984.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2F656d7ff7-734d-4563-8852-2bc3b4723fcf_250x307.jpeg)

![](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!_iCd!,w_1456,c_limit,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fbucketeer-e05bbc84-baa3-437e-9518-adb32be77984.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2F656d7ff7-734d-4563-8852-2bc3b4723fcf_250x307.jpeg)

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

[](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!ks9l!,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fbucketeer-e05bbc84-baa3-437e-9518-adb32be77984.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2F0a4afd5d-4e4f-4788-9d39-56084db00244_250x195.jpeg)

![](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!ks9l!,w_1456,c_limit,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fbucketeer-e05bbc84-baa3-437e-9518-adb32be77984.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2F0a4afd5d-4e4f-4788-9d39-56084db00244_250x195.jpeg)

[](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!Rn__!,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fbucketeer-e05bbc84-baa3-437e-9518-adb32be77984.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2F9b615efc-7fb1-4617-98ae-28a8680c3eec_250x179.jpeg)

![](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!Rn__!,w_1456,c_limit,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fbucketeer-e05bbc84-baa3-437e-9518-adb32be77984.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2F9b615efc-7fb1-4617-98ae-28a8680c3eec_250x179.jpeg)

As per the Sthala Purana, there was a grand Vaishnava Yagna in the Himalayas once. The Gandharvas under Chitragriva too attended it, however attracted by the beauty of the Sabarakantha ladies, they tried to molest them. Angered at their conduct Maharshi Vamadeva cursed the Gandharvas to be re born as Sabaras. Chitragreeva was born as a Sabara chieftain, his second wife Chitkala was a devout Shaivaite. However his jealous first wife Chitti, caused Chitkala to be banished.

Chitragreeva asked Chitkala to collect flowers from 2 branches of the Madhuka tree, and make a living with it. Obeying his orders, she daily collected the flowers. However due to her devotion to Shiva,those flowers turned into golden flowers, which she would sell. When Chitti again began to be jealous of Chitkala’s prosperity, Chitragreeva began to cut down the Madhuka tree, and Shiva appeared in the Rudra form, and soon freed the Gandharvas from their curse. Hence he is called as Madhukeshwara.

The main Lingam in Srimukhalingam is carved out of the logs of the Madhuka tree. It is believed that Shiva’s face is visible on the logs, which also gives this place it’s name.

The *Madhuka* tree (commonly *Mahua*, *Madhuca indica*) is cherished by tribal communities across central and eastern Indi,for its flowers, shade, and ritual significance. That it becomes the material for Shiva’s visage is profoundly meaningful.

Though now in ruins, the Bhimeshwara shrine has the presence of Chaturmukha Dakshinamurthy , Shiva as the supreme teacher with four faces may symbolize wisdom flowing in all directions *Jnana (knowledge), Vairagya (detachment), Aishwarya*

The temple’s crumbling stone might mirror time’s erasure, but this sacred alignment still hums with metaphysical intent. Though it retains only the garbha griha, the towering shikhara stands sentinel over the Someshwara Templ, like a vertical prayer frozen in stone.

The monolithic nature of the sculptures elevates the temple’s aesthetic to near-miraculous levels.Each relief feels like it was *grown* from the stone, not chiseled onto it, suggesting incredible craftsmanship and spiritual intention.It aligns with the ancient Indian philosophy of non-duality, where *form and material* are one, just as Shiva is both the image and the infinite.

For quite some time, the Srimukhalingam Temple complex was in neglect, before the Parlakimidi Maharaja of the Gajapati dynasty renovated them 200 years back. From then on,the Parlakimidi Royal family has been taking care of the temple well. The family also oversees the Mahashivratri celebrations at Srimukhalingam. Celebrated for 9 dayes here, many devotees not just from Andhra,but also neighbouring Odisha visit this temple during that time.

Srimukhalingam is reachable from the district HQ of Srikakulam, and Vishakapatnam is the nearest airport. Do visit this wonderful temple for it’s architecture,the sculptures ,the picturesque surroundings,the Nagavali river nearby and the unique Lingam made out of wood.
